meta.title: Attention & Perception Science for SOTY Event Horizon
meta.author: Cleanlystudio
meta.description: Comprehensive attention science database covering eye-tracking, perception, cognitive laws, and scroll behavior

f_pattern.description: Users scan the top of the page horizontally, move down, scan a shorter horizontal line, then scan the left side vertically
f_pattern.shape: Two horizontal stripes and a vertical stripe forming an F
f_pattern.applies_to: Text-heavy pages, search results, news articles, documentation
f_pattern.relevance_to_event_horizon: LOW — F-pattern assumes text-dominant layouts. Event Horizon is visual-first.
f_pattern.key_insight: Users rarely read word-by-word on the web. They scan. Even narrative text gets scanned first.

z_pattern.description: Eye moves from top-left → top-right → diagonal to bottom-left → bottom-right
z_pattern.applies_to: Simple layouts with minimal text, landing pages, advertisements
z_pattern.relevance_to_event_horizon: MEDIUM — useful for individual chapter screens where title (top) leads to CTA/next section (bottom-right)
z_pattern.key_insight: The diagonal sweep is where the eye is least focused — place less critical elements here.

gutenberg_diagram.description: Page divided into four quadrants based on natural reading gravity

primary_optical_area.attention: Highest — first place the eye goes

terminal_area.attention: Medium — natural resting point after scanning

gutenberg_diagram.relevance_to_event_horizon: LOW-MEDIUM — most relevant for HUD layout. Place critical data in primary optical area, navigation in terminal area.

center_bias.description: In full-screen visual experiences, viewers fixate on the center first and most frequently
center_bias.research: Tatler (2007) — 'The central fixation bias in scene viewing: Selecting an optimal viewing position independently of motor biases and image feature distributions'
center_bias.relevance_to_event_horizon: HIGHEST — the black hole at center leverages the strongest natural fixation tendency. The most important element is where people already look.
center_bias.key_insight: Don't fight the center bias — use it. Place the black hole center or near-center. Everything else orbits this natural fixation point.

saccade_behavior.description: Saccades are rapid eye movements between fixation points. The eye does NOT move smoothly — it jumps from point to point.

characteristics.speed: Up to 900°/second — one of the fastest movements the human body makes
characteristics.frequency: 3-5 saccades per second during active viewing
characteristics.suppression: Vision is suppressed during saccades — you are effectively blind during the jump (saccadic suppression)
characteristics.amplitude: Average saccade covers 2-5° of visual angle (about 2-5cm at screen distance)

implications_for_design: Elements need to be different enough from their neighbors to attract saccadic targeting (visual saliency)
Animation that only lasts during a saccade (< 50ms) will literally be invisible to the viewer
Smooth pursuit eye movement (following a moving object) only works if the object moves at < 30°/second
Very fast animations may be missed if they occur during a saccade — keep important animations > 200ms

fixation_points.description: Fixations are the pauses between saccades where the eye actually processes visual information

characteristics.visual_acuity: Sharp vision only within the fovea — central 2° of visual field (about 2cm at arm's length)
characteristics.processing: Most visual processing happens during fixations, not saccades

high_contrast_edges.explanation: Edges where light meets dark are the primary target for fixations

faces_and_eyes.explanation: Even abstract face-like patterns attract fixation (pareidolia)

text.explanation: Literate viewers automatically fixate on readable text

motion.explanation: Motion in peripheral vision triggers reflexive saccade to the movement source

color_singularities.explanation: A single colored element among neutral ones attracts fixation (pop-out effect)

semantic_relevance.explanation: Elements related to the viewer's current goal/task attract fixation

event_horizon_implications: The boundary of the black hole (edge between void and accretion disk) will be a natural fixation magnet — HIGH contrast edge
Text elements will automatically attract fixation — position them deliberately so they don't compete with the visual
Moving particles attract peripheral fixation — use this to guide the eye toward specific areas
A single bright star or HUD element can redirect attention — use sparingly

peripheral_vision.description: Vision outside the fovea (central 2°). Low resolution but highly sensitive to motion and contrast.

resolution_drop.20_degrees: 4% of central acuity (can barely read large text)

what_peripheral_vision_detects: Motion (VERY sensitive — primary function is detecting approaching threats)
Color (reduced but present — warm colors detected better peripherally)
what_peripheral_vision_cannot_detect: Fine text (below ~24px at normal viewing distance at > 10°)
design_implications: Particles in the periphery don't need high detail — just motion and color blobs
Text must be positioned where the eye will foveate (center or natural fixation zones)
Use peripheral motion to guide attention — a subtle flash in the corner draws the eye without being conscious
The 'glow' around the accretion disk leverages peripheral color detection — creates atmosphere even when not directly looked at

change_blindness.description: People fail to detect large changes in visual scenes when the change coincides with a brief disruption (blink, saccade, cut, or occlusion)
change_blindness.classic_experiment: Simons & Levin (1997) — person asking for directions is replaced by different person during brief obstruction. 50% of participants didn't notice.
implications: You can change significant visual elements during scroll transitions without the viewer noticing — they won't see changes that happen during their blink/saccade
Scene composition can shift dramatically between chapters if the transition includes a visual disruption (fade to black, flash)
Use this to your advantage: swap particle systems, change post-processing, modify the black hole during moment of darkness
BUT: if you WANT the viewer to notice a change, make it happen while they're looking — no disruption, smooth transition

inattentional_blindness.description: People fail to perceive fully visible objects when their attention is focused elsewhere
inattentional_blindness.classic_experiment: Simons & Chabris (1999) — 'The Invisible Gorilla'. 50% of viewers focused on counting basketball passes failed to see a gorilla walk through the scene.
implications: When the viewer is focused on reading text, they may completely miss visual changes in the 3D scene
Don't rely on background visual cues to communicate important information — the viewer might not see them
Important state changes need to be in the viewer's focus area or preceded by attention-directing cues
For Event Horizon: when narrative text is active, don't assume the viewer is watching the black hole. Stage visual reveals for AFTER text reading.

banner_blindness.description: Users have learned to ignore areas of the page that typically contain ads or non-content (headers, sidebars, cookie banners)
banner_blindness.research: Benway & Lane (1998) — coined the term. Users ignored the most prominent elements on the page because they looked like banners.
relevance: HUD elements positioned in typical 'header' or 'sidebar' zones may be ignored
Navigation elements need to feel integrated into the experience, not like standard web chrome
Chapter indicators should NOT look like a generic website progress bar — they'll be mentally filtered out
Cookie/consent banners should be handled BEFORE the experience starts, not overlaid on top of the immersive content

visual_saliency.description: The quality that makes an element stand out from its surroundings and attract bottom-up (involuntary) attention

contrast.description: The difference in luminance between an element and its surround. Higher contrast = higher saliency.
contrast.event_horizon: On a near-black background, even a dim element has extremely high Weber contrast. This means every light pixel matters — be intentional.

color.description: Chromatic uniqueness. A single colored element among achromatic ones is maximally salient.
color.measurement: Color distance in CIE-Lab color space between element and surround
color.event_horizon: The cyan HUD on black background has extreme color saliency. Use this power wisely — don't put cyan on everything or nothing will stand out.

motion.description: Movement against a static background. Detected by specialized motion-sensitive neurons (V5/MT area).
motion.characteristics: Motion onset is most salient (the moment something STARTS moving). Continuous motion habituates (becomes less salient over time).
motion.event_horizon: Particle motion is constantly present → habituates. A NEW motion (text reveal, camera movement, flash) is highly salient because it breaks the pattern.

orientation.description: Elements tilted differently from their surroundings. A diagonal line among horizontals pops out.
orientation.event_horizon: Most elements orbit horizontally. A vertical or diagonal element (like text or a data line) stands out.

size.description: Larger elements are more salient but diminishing returns above a certain size.
size.event_horizon: The black hole is the largest element → highest size saliency. Text must be large enough to compete with the visual backdrop.

faces.description: Human brains have specialized face-detection circuitry (fusiform face area). Faces ALWAYS attract attention.
faces.event_horizon: No faces in Event Horizon — but this means nothing will compete with faces for attention. Good: the experience won't be fighting biology.

saliency_hierarchy_for_event_horizon: 1. The black hole boundary (extreme luminance contrast edge)
2. Text (learned saliency — literate brains automatically fixate on text)
3. HUD elements (color saliency — cyan on black)
6. Background particles (continuous motion — habituated, low saliency)

common_claim.claim: Human attention span is 8 seconds, shorter than a goldfish
common_claim.reality: MISLEADING. This measures selective attention switching, not sustained engagement. It does NOT mean people can't focus for more than 8 seconds.

selective_attention.description: Ability to focus on one stimulus while ignoring others
selective_attention.duration: Variable — can switch in < 1 second if more salient stimulus appears
selective_attention.relevance: Users will switch from your content to a notification instantly. You must be more interesting than their phone.

sustained_attention.description: Ability to maintain focus on a single task over time
sustained_attention.duration: 10-20 minutes for compelling content before needing variety or a break
sustained_attention.relevance: Event Horizon should target 5-10 minutes maximum. Include pacing variety to refresh sustained attention.
factors_that_extend: Narrative momentum (wanting to know what happens next)

executive_attention.description: Ability to regulate attention, resolve conflict, and maintain goals
executive_attention.relevance: Don't require the user to make decisions. Scrolling should be the only action required.

web_specific_attention_data.average_page_visit: 52 seconds (Hubspot, 2023) — but this includes all page types
web_specific_attention_data.bounce_rate_threshold: If no engagement signal in 3 seconds, 53% of mobile users leave (Google)
web_specific_attention_data.above_fold_time: 57% of viewing time is above the fold (Nielsen, 2018)
web_specific_attention_data.scroll_depth_average: Only 50% of users reach 60% of a standard article
web_specific_attention_data.immersive_experience_exception: SOTY-level immersive sites see 3-5 minute average sessions with 70-90% scroll completion

immersive_experience_sites.note: Significantly higher completion rates due to engagement and novelty

factors_that_increase_scroll_completion: Visual change on every scroll interaction (no 'dead zones')
Chapter/snap points (sense of achievement at each chapter)
Audio that evolves with scroll (creates continuity)
Fast initial load (under 3 seconds to interactive)
factors_that_cause_scroll_abandonment: Long sections without visual change ('scroll desert')
Confusing navigation (am I scrolling the right way?)
Audio issues (autoplay annoyance, volume too loud)
Content that doesn't deliver on the initial visual promise
Mobile usability problems (too small text, hard to scroll)

strategies: Every 5% of scroll should produce a noticeable visual change
Chapter transitions act as micro-rewards that encourage continued scrolling
The 'what's beyond the event horizon?' question creates powerful curiosity-driven scroll motivation
Progress indicators show the viewer they're on a journey with a destination

variables.T: Time to acquire target (move pointer to it)
variables.D: Distance from current pointer position to target center
variables.W: Width of target along the axis of movement

fitts_law.simplified_rule: Make clickable targets LARGE and CLOSE to likely cursor positions

specific_values.edge_advantage: Elements at screen edges have 'infinite width' in one direction — effectively easier to hit

event_horizon_application: Interactive HUD elements: minimum 44×44px touch target (even if visual is smaller, the hit area should be larger)
Chapter navigation dots: at least 24×24px visual, 44×44px hit area
Any clickable link or button overlaying the WebGL canvas: high contrast + sufficient size
Screen-edge navigation (arrows, progress bar) benefits from edge advantage

hicks_law.simplified_rule: More choices = slower decisions = more cognitive load = more abandonment
event_horizon_application: Don't present the user with choices during the experience. Scrolling should be the ONLY action.
Navigation should be simple: chapters listed linearly, not in a complex menu
If any interactive moment exists, limit to 1-2 options maximum
The 'start experience' moment should be a single, obvious action (scroll or click ONE button)
Audio toggle: single mute/unmute button — not a volume slider with multiple options

millers_law.rule: The average person can hold 7 ± 2 chunks of information in working memory
millers_law.modern_revision: Cowan (2001) revised this to 4 ± 1 chunks for focused attention
millers_law.chunking: Information grouped into meaningful clusters reduces cognitive load. 1-800-555-1234 is easier than 18005551234.
event_horizon_application: Each chapter should present 3-5 key concepts maximum — not 10-15 facts dumped at once
HUD data displays: limit to 4-5 visible data points at a time. Rotate or reveal more progressively.
Scientific data should be chunked: 'Mass: 6.5 billion suns' is one chunk. Don't present mass, radius, temperature, spin, age, luminosity simultaneously.
Navigation: maximum 5-7 chapter labels visible. If more chapters, use progressive disclosure.

weber_fechner_law.description: The just-noticeable difference (JND) in a stimulus is proportional to the magnitude of the stimulus
weber_fechner_law.formula: ΔS/S = k (where ΔS is the change, S is the original stimulus, k is a constant)
weber_fechner_law.implication: The stronger the stimulus, the larger the change needed to be noticed

practical_values.brightness: JND ≈ 1-2% change in luminance for focused viewing, 5-10% for peripheral
practical_values.size: JND ≈ 3-5% change in object size to be noticeable
practical_values.speed: JND ≈ 8-10% change in animation speed to be perceived
practical_values.color: JND varies by hue — blue is least sensitive (~15° hue shift), red-green most sensitive (~2-3° hue shift)

event_horizon_application: Scroll-driven size changes must exceed 3-5% per noticeable step to register
Gradual color transitions need to be spread over enough scroll distance for the total change to be above JND at each step
Audio volume changes must exceed 1-2 dB to be perceptible — gradual 0.5dB changes will be imperceptible
Ambient 'breathing' effects (pulsing glow, particle density) should vary by at least 5-10% to be subconsciously felt

von_restorff_effect.description: The isolation effect — an item that is distinctly different from its surroundings is more likely to be remembered
von_restorff_effect.mechanism: Distinctive items receive more attention encoding and are stored more vividly in memory
event_horizon_application: The moment of crossing the event horizon should be VISUALLY DISTINCT from everything else — a moment that stands out in memory
Key facts should be presented differently from surrounding text (different size, color, or animation)
One visually unique element per chapter anchors the memory of that chapter
The silence moment (after the crossing) is a von Restorff isolate — it's the ONLY silence in the experience

peak_end_rule.description: People judge an experience based on how they felt at its most intense point (peak) and at its end, rather than the average of every moment
peak_end_rule.classic_experiment: Cold water experiment — longer painful experience judged better if it ended with slightly less pain
implications: The PEAK moment (crossing the event horizon) must be the most emotionally intense moment — this defines the memory of the whole experience
The ENDING must be satisfying — a weak ending poisons the memory of even an amazing experience
A mediocre middle section is forgiven if the peak and end are exceptional
The last 30 seconds of the experience are disproportionately important to the overall impression

event_horizon_application.peak: The crossing moment — maximum audio/visual intensity followed by total void. This is the moment people will remember and describe to others.
event_horizon_application.end: The epilogue must resolve emotionally. Return to stars, contemplative tone, sense of completion. End with beauty, not with a whimper.
event_horizon_application.design_priority: If time/budget is limited, invest disproportionately in the peak and ending moments.

zeigarnik_effect.description: People remember uncompleted or interrupted tasks better than completed ones. Incompleteness creates cognitive tension.
event_horizon_application: The experience should always hint at what's coming next — creating a sense of incompleteness that drives scrolling
Chapter endings should tease the next chapter: 'But what happens at the event horizon?' creates Zeigarnik tension
The final chapter should provide closure to resolve the accumulated Zeigarnik tension
Loading screen: show partial elements of the experience to create tension and anticipation

dwell_time_and_engagement.description: How long users spend looking at and interacting with specific elements

bounce_rate.definition: Percentage of visitors who leave without interacting

average_session_duration.definition: Mean time spent on the experience
average_session_duration.benchmark: Average web page: 52 seconds. Immersive experiences: 2-5 minutes.

scroll_completion.definition: Percentage of visitors who reach the end

return_visits.definition: Percentage of visitors who come back

cognitive_load_theory.description: Working memory has limited capacity. Cognitive load is the total amount of mental effort used. Three types of load.

intrinsic_load.description: Inherent difficulty of the content itself (black hole physics IS complex)
intrinsic_load.management: Use visual metaphors to simplify. Don't explain spaghettification in words — SHOW it. One concept per chapter.

extraneous_load.description: Load caused by poor design — confusing layout, unclear navigation, unnecessary information
extraneous_load.management: Minimize: clean layout, clear typography, obvious navigation, no clutter. Every extraneous element wastes cognitive bandwidth.

germane_load.description: Load used for learning and understanding — this is the GOOD load
germane_load.management: Maximize: design the experience to promote understanding. Visual demonstrations > text explanations. Build on prior knowledge progressively.

event_horizon_application: Each chapter should manage intrinsic load by presenting ONE concept clearly
Eliminate all extraneous load: no unnecessary UI, no confusing interactions, no competing visual elements
Promote germane load: visual demonstrations of physics concepts that build understanding
The experience teaches through showing, not telling — visual learning has lower intrinsic load than textual learning
